notes.note: Cicero's brother Quintus, whose son tells contradictory stories to each estranged parent; "his father" is Quintus, "his mother" is Pomponia, Atticus's sister.
Cicero echoes the Pindar quotation of the previous letter (Greek skolia, "crooked"): Atticus has advised handling the nephew by indirection rather than open confrontation.

A treatise by Phaedrus the Epicurean (head of the Athens school, c. 70 BC), whom Cicero had heard as a young man; he wants the book for his own philosophical writing. "On the Gods" renders the Greek peri Theon (theon = "of the gods"), the title's subject, not a person's name.
